# MPEP Key Sections — Operative Tests and Standards

> **Source & copyright:** The Manual of Patent Examining Procedure (MPEP) and the underlying U.S. patent statutes (Title 35 U.S.C.) and rules (37 CFR) are **works of the U.S. Government and are in the public domain** (17 U.S.C. § 105). They are free to reproduce. This file summarizes the *operative test / standard* for each section and links to the authoritative USPTO source so the full text can be consulted directly.
>
> **Authoritative publisher:** U.S. Patent and Trademark Office — MPEP (current edition), https://www.uspto.gov/web/offices/pac/mpep/
>
> **Compiled:** 2026-06-15. Each section's operative requirement below was summarized from the cited USPTO page (fetched live unless marked "(verify against source)").

---

## 608 / 608.01 — Specification and Claims

- **Section:** MPEP 608, 608.01 (and 37 CFR 1.71–1.75)
- **Title:** Disclosure; The Specification; Claims
- **URL:** https://www.uspto.gov/web/offices/pac/mpep/s608.html

**Operative requirement.** Under 37 CFR 1.71(a) the specification must describe the invention in "full, clear, concise, and exact terms" sufficient to enable a person skilled in the art to make and use it; it must set forth the manner of making and using, the best mode contemplated, and distinguish the invention from prior art. Claims (37 CFR 1.75) must particularly point out and distinctly claim the subject matter, begin on a separate sheet, and each claim is a single sentence directed to one aspect. The **no-new-matter doctrine** governs throughout: claims and amendments must find support (express, implicit, or inherent) in the originally filed disclosure; post-filing introduction of previously undisclosed subject matter is prohibited.

---

## 2106 — Patent Subject Matter Eligibility (35 U.S.C. § 101)

- **Section:** MPEP 2106
- **Title:** Patent Subject Matter Eligibility
- **URL:** https://www.uspto.gov/web/offices/pac/mpep/s2106.html

**Operative test (USPTO implementation of the *Alice/Mayo* framework).**

- **Step 1 — Statutory category:** Is the claim to a process, machine, manufacture, or composition of matter? If not, it is non-statutory and ineligible.
- **Step 2A, Prong One:** Does the claim *recite* a judicial exception — an abstract idea, a law of nature, or a natural phenomenon (i.e., is the exception "set forth" or "described" in the claim)? If no, the claim is eligible.
- **Step 2A, Prong Two:** If yes, does the claim integrate the recited exception into a **practical application** (i.e., apply, rely on, or use it in a meaningful way beyond merely linking it to a technological environment)? If yes, the claim is eligible.
- **Step 2B — "Significantly more":** If the exception is not integrated, do the additional elements (individually and in combination) amount to **significantly more** than the exception — an "inventive concept" that is not well-understood, routine, and conventional? If yes, eligible; if no, ineligible.

---

## 2131 — Anticipation (35 U.S.C. § 102)

- **Section:** MPEP 2131
- **Title:** Anticipation — Application of 35 U.S.C. 102
- **URL:** https://www.uspto.gov/web/offices/pac/mpep/s2131.html

**Operative standard.** A claim is anticipated when **a single prior art reference** discloses **each and every element** of the claimed invention, **arranged as required by the claim** ("the identical invention . . . in as complete detail as is contained in the . . . claim"). Identical terminology is not required. An element not expressly disclosed may still be **inherent** if extrinsic evidence makes clear that the missing matter is *necessarily present* in the reference and would be so recognized by a person of ordinary skill; the reference need not have recognized the inherent property at the time.

---

## 2141 — Obviousness: Graham Factors and the KSR Framework (35 U.S.C. § 103)

- **Section:** MPEP 2141
- **Title:** Examination Guidelines for Determining Obviousness Under 35 U.S.C. 103
- **URL:** https://www.uspto.gov/web/offices/pac/mpep/s2141.html

**Operative test.** Obviousness rests on the four factual inquiries of *Graham v. John Deere*:
1. the scope and content of the prior art;
2. the differences between the prior art and the claims at issue;
3. the level of ordinary skill in the pertinent art; and
4. objective indicia / secondary considerations (commercial success, long-felt but unmet need, failure of others, unexpected results, copying, etc.).

Per *KSR v. Teleflex*, the analysis is **flexible** and the rigid teaching-suggestion-motivation (TSM) test is not the exclusive route. A person of ordinary skill is presumed to have **ordinary creativity and common sense**; combining familiar elements by known methods is likely obvious when it yields only **predictable results**. The examiner must articulate **explicit factual findings and reasoning** (no conclusory statements) explaining why the claimed invention would have been obvious.

---

## 2143 — Exemplary Rationales Supporting Obviousness (post-KSR)

- **Section:** MPEP 2143
- **Title:** Examples of Basic Requirements of a Prima Facie Case of Obviousness
- **URL:** https://www.uspto.gov/web/offices/pac/mpep/s2143.html

**Operative standard — the seven exemplary rationales (A–G).** Any one may support a § 103 rejection if the underlying factual findings are articulated:
- **(A)** Combining prior art elements according to known methods to yield predictable results.
- **(B)** Simple substitution of one known element for another to obtain predictable results.
- **(C)** Use of a known technique to improve similar devices (methods, products) in the same way.
- **(D)** Applying a known technique to a known device (method, product) ready for improvement to yield predictable results.
- **(E)** "Obvious to try" — choosing from a finite number of identified, predictable solutions with a reasonable expectation of success.
- **(F)** Known work in one field of endeavor prompting variations in the same or a different field based on design incentives or market forces, where the variation is predictable.
- **(G)** Teaching, suggestion, or motivation (TSM) in the prior art that would have led one of ordinary skill to modify the reference or combine teachings to arrive at the claimed invention.

In every case, mere conclusory statements are insufficient; the rationale must be supported by articulated findings.

---

## 2161 — The Three Separate Requirements of 35 U.S.C. § 112(a)

- **Section:** MPEP 2161
- **Title:** Three Separate Requirements for Specification Under 35 U.S.C. 112(a)
- **URL:** https://www.uspto.gov/web/offices/pac/mpep/s2161.html

**Operative standard.** Section 112(a) imposes **three separate and independent** requirements: (1) **written description** — show the inventor was in possession of the claimed invention as of the filing date; (2) **enablement** — teach how to make and use the full scope of the claimed invention without undue experimentation; and (3) **best mode** — do not conceal the inventor's preferred mode of carrying out the invention. The requirements are distinct: a disclosure may satisfy one yet fail another (e.g., a claim can be described without being enabled, and vice versa).

---

## 2163 — Written Description Requirement (35 U.S.C. § 112(a))

- **Section:** MPEP 2163
- **Title:** Guidelines for the Examination of Patent Applications Under the 35 U.S.C. 112(a) Written Description Requirement
- **URL:** https://www.uspto.gov/web/offices/pac/mpep/s2163.html

**Operative test.** The specification must **convey with reasonable clarity to a person skilled in the art that the inventor was in possession** of the claimed invention as of the filing date. **Original claims** enjoy a presumption of adequate description (rebuttable by examiner evidence). For **new or amended claims**, the applicant must show support in the original disclosure (express, implicit, or inherent). For a **genus**, adequate written description requires either a **representative number of species** spanning the genus's variation or **disclosure of structural features common to the genus** (or a known structure-function correlation); a single species supports a genus only where one skilled in the art would recognize the genus is described. The analysis is fact-specific, turning on the nature of the invention and the state of the art.

---

## 2164 — Enablement Requirement (35 U.S.C. § 112(a))

- **Section:** MPEP 2164
- **Title:** The Enablement Requirement
- **URL:** https://www.uspto.gov/web/offices/pac/mpep/s2164.html

**Operative test.** The specification must enable a person skilled in the art to **make and use the full scope** of the claimed invention **without undue experimentation**. Whether experimentation is "undue" is assessed by weighing **all** of the *In re Wands* factors together (no single factor is dispositive):
1. breadth of the claims;
2. nature of the invention;
3. state of the prior art;
4. level of ordinary skill;
5. level of predictability in the art;
6. amount of direction/guidance provided by the inventor;
7. presence or absence of working examples; and
8. quantity of experimentation needed to make/use the invention based on the disclosure.

The required disclosure varies inversely with predictability — unpredictable / nascent arts demand more specific and detailed teaching.

---

## 2173 / 2173.05 — Definiteness; Terms of Degree (35 U.S.C. § 112(b))

- **Section:** MPEP 2173, 2173.05 (incl. 2173.05(b) relative terms / terms of degree)
- **Title:** Claims Must Particularly Point Out and Distinctly Claim the Invention
- **URL:** https://www.uspto.gov/web/offices/pac/mpep/s2173.html

**Operative test.** Claims must **particularly point out and distinctly claim** the subject matter. During examination, applying the **broadest reasonable interpretation** in light of the specification, a claim is **indefinite** if it contains words or phrases whose meaning is unclear such that a person of ordinary skill cannot ascertain the **metes and bounds** of the claim with reasonable certainty. **Terms of degree / relative terms** ("substantially," "about," "approximately") are permissible **only if the specification (or the art) provides a standard for measuring that degree** so a skilled artisan can determine the scope. Note the distinction: **breadth is not indefiniteness** — a broad genus claim is definite if its boundaries are clear. Examiners must identify the problematic language, explain why it is indefinite, and create a clear prosecution record.

---

## 2181–2186 — Means-Plus-Function Claiming; Williamson (35 U.S.C. § 112(f))

- **Section:** MPEP 2181–2186 (principal interpretive standard at 2181)
- **Title:** Identifying and Interpreting a 35 U.S.C. 112(f) (or pre-AIA 112, sixth paragraph) Limitation
- **URL:** https://www.uspto.gov/web/offices/pac/mpep/s2181.html

**Operative test.** A limitation invokes § 112(f) when it satisfies a **three-prong analysis**: (1) it uses "means" / "step" **or** a non-structural generic placeholder ("mechanism for," "module for," "device for," "unit for," "configured to"); (2) the placeholder is modified by **functional language** (typically introduced by "for" or "configured to"); and (3) the placeholder is **not modified by sufficient structure, material, or acts** to perform the recited function. Use of "means" creates a presumption that § 112(f) applies; absence of "means" creates the opposite presumption. Under ***Williamson v. Citrix***, the no-"means" presumption is **rebutted** when the claim term fails to recite sufficiently definite structure (i.e., the term is not understood by skilled artisans as a name for structure). When § 112(f) applies, the specification must disclose **corresponding structure clearly linked** to the function; for **computer-implemented** functions, a **specific algorithm** must be disclosed (a general-purpose computer alone is insufficient). Failure to disclose adequate corresponding structure renders the claim **indefinite under § 112(b)**.

---

## 211–216 — Claiming Benefit of an Earlier U.S. Filing Date (35 U.S.C. §§ 119(e), 120, 121, 365(c))

- **Section:** MPEP 211–216 (entry point at 211)
- **Title:** Claiming the Benefit of an Earlier Filing Date Under 35 U.S.C. 120 and 119(e)
- **URL:** https://www.uspto.gov/web/offices/pac/mpep/s211.html

**Operative requirements.** To claim benefit of a prior U.S. (provisional or nonprovisional) application:
1. **Specific reference** to the prior application in an **Application Data Sheet** (37 CFR 1.76) for applications filed on/after Sept. 16, 2012, identifying the application/provisional number and the relationship (continuation, divisional, or continuation-in-part).
2. **Copendency** — the later application must be filed before the patenting, abandonment, or termination of proceedings on the prior application (35 U.S.C. § 120).
3. **Common inventor** — at least one inventor in common with the prior application.
4. **Timeliness** — for utility nonprovisionals, the reference must be made within the later of 4 months from actual filing or 16 months from the prior application's filing date (37 CFR 1.78); late claims require an unintentional-delay petition and fee.
5. **§ 112(a) support** — the prior application must disclose the later-claimed invention in compliance with § 112(a) (written description and enablement; best mode excepted) for the claims to receive its benefit.

A claim is entitled to the earlier date **only for subject matter supported under § 112(a)** in the prior application; claim-by-claim analysis applies (esp. for continuations-in-part).

---

## Chapter 1500 — Design Patents (35 U.S.C. § 171)

- **Section:** MPEP Chapter 1500 (definition at 1502; examination at 1504)
- **Title:** Design Patents — Definition; Examination
- **URL (definition):** https://www.uspto.gov/web/offices/pac/mpep/s1502.html
- **URL (examination):** https://www.uspto.gov/web/offices/pac/mpep/s1504.html

**Operative requirements.** A design patent protects the **ornamental (non-functional) appearance** — configuration/shape, surface ornamentation, or both — **embodied in or applied to an article of manufacture**; the design cannot exist as abstract, free-floating ornamentation. A design application contains a **single claim**, and the **drawings constitute the disclosure and effectively define the claim** (they must fully and definitely disclose the design). Examination requires five things:
- **Ornamentality (§ 171):** the design must be ornamental and **not dictated solely by function**; the examiner may establish a prima facie functional case (rebuttable by evidence of ornamental design choices).
- **Novelty (§ 102):** anticipation under the **ordinary observer test** — whether an ordinary observer, giving the attention of a purchaser, would be deceived into believing the claimed design is the same as the prior art design, judged on the overall appearance.
- **Nonobviousness (§ 103):** requires a **primary reference** whose design characteristics are **basically the same** as the claimed design, optionally modified by secondary references, from the perspective of a designer of ordinary skill.
- **Enablement / Definiteness (§ 112):** the drawings must enable and definitely show the design as embodied in the article.

**Term:** 15 years from grant for design applications filed on or after May 13, 2015 (14 years for earlier-filed). *(verify against source — term figure stated from authoritative knowledge, not directly quoted from the fetched pages.)*

---

## 2001 — Duty of Disclosure / Duty of Candor and Good Faith (37 CFR 1.56)

- **Section:** MPEP 2001
- **Title:** Duty of Disclosure, Candor, and Good Faith
- **URL:** https://www.uspto.gov/web/offices/pac/mpep/s2001.html

**Operative requirement.** Each individual associated with the filing and prosecution of an application — including each named inventor, each attorney/agent, and every other person substantively involved — owes the USPTO a **duty of candor and good faith**, which includes a **duty to disclose all information known to that individual to be material to patentability**. Information is **material** when it is **not cumulative** to information already of record and it either (1) establishes, alone or in combination, a prima facie case of unpatentability, or (2) refutes or is inconsistent with a position the applicant takes in opposing a rejection or asserting patentability. The duty **continues throughout prosecution** (until abandonment or grant), and material information may be submitted even after allowance, before payment of the issue fee. There is **no duty to conduct a search**, but known material information must be disclosed. Breach can render the patent unenforceable (inequitable conduct).
